citation: Sim, M., Thornton, H. R., Duhig, S. J., Whiteley, R., Timmins, R. G., & Bourne, M. N. (2023). Hamstring strain injury risk factors in Australian football change over the course of the season. Medicine & Science in Sports & Exercise, 55(10), 1837–1847. https://doi.org/10.1249/MSS.0000000000003297
abstract: This prospective cohort study followed 311 AFL players across an entire competitive season to identify how hamstring strain injury (HSI) risk factors change from pre-season through early and late in-season periods. Assessments included eccentric hamstring strength (NordBord), biceps femoris long head (BFlh) muscle architecture via ultrasound (fascicle length, pennation angle, muscle thickness), and prior HSI history. Multiple logistic regression with recursive feature elimination identified the best-fit predictors for each time epoch. Pre-season HSI risk was dominated by non-modifiable factors (prior HSI, age, games played), yielding high predictive accuracy (AUC = 0.83). Early in-season risk was predicted by modifiable BFlh architectural variables—specifically pennation angle and fascicle length—with excellent accuracy (AUC = 0.86). Late in-season risk was essentially unpredictable (AUC = 0.46), suggesting fatigue and accumulated load dominate at this stage.
tldr: HSI risk prediction in AFL players is highly time-dependent: pre-season risk is best captured by injury history and non-modifiable factors, early in-season risk by BFlh muscle architecture, and late in-season risk is largely unpredictable by standard assessments. Practitioners should shift their risk screening strategies as the season progresses rather than applying a single static model year-round.
methods: Prospective cohort design with 311 male AFL players across one full season. Baseline assessments: NordBord eccentric hamstring strength, BFlh ultrasound (fascicle length, pennation angle, muscle thickness), and injury history. Logistic regression with recursive feature elimination applied separately for pre-season, early in-season (rounds 1–11), and late in-season (rounds 12–22) HSI prediction. Model performance evaluated via AUC–ROC. Injury defined as any HSI resulting in missed training or competition.
findings: Pre-season model (AUC = 0.83): prior HSI, age, and total career games were the strongest predictors. Early in-season model (AUC = 0.86): BFlh pennation angle and fascicle length were the primary predictors—players with smaller fascicle length and greater pennation angle were at higher risk. Late in-season model (AUC = 0.46): no assessment combination yielded meaningful predictive accuracy. HSI incidence was highest in the late in-season phase despite the inability to predict it from standard metrics.
limitations: Single-season design from one AFL club limits generalizability. Ultrasound measures reflect structural status at one time point and may not capture within-season changes. Accumulated training load and fatigue metrics were not included as predictors, which may explain the late in-season prediction failure. Sample size for some sub-groups was modest.
practicalImplications: Practitioners should use injury history and career exposure data to tier pre-season risk. Early in-season, BFlh ultrasound screening provides actionable targets—strengthening programs to increase fascicle length (e.g., Nordic hamstring exercise) should be prioritized for flagged athletes. Acknowledge that late in-season HSI risk cannot be reliably predicted and shift strategy toward workload management and monitoring instead.
athleteDev: For American football development, this study supports periodic BFlh ultrasound as a practical tool during pre-season camps and early in the competitive season to identify athletes with architectural deficits. Integrating Nordic hamstring exercises and hip extension loading into off-season and early pre-season programming may structurally adapt the BFlh to reduce risk during the competitive window.
rtp: Athletes returning from prior HSI should be flagged as elevated risk throughout the entire season, not just immediately post-clearance. BFlh architecture (fascicle length and pennation angle) measured via ultrasound should be assessed before RTP clearance and tracked in follow-up assessments. If BFlh fascicle length has not normalized post-injury, re-injury risk remains elevated even after clinical strength benchmarks are met.

citation: Riego Ruiz, C., Bores Cerezal, A., Urtasun Longás, B., Pérez García, N., Martínez Pernía, D., Castaño Zambudio, A., & de Villarreal, E. S. (2026). Hamstring Training and Football Performance: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis. International Journal of Sports Medicine. https://doi.org/10.1055/a-2785-7739
abstract: This PRISMA-compliant systematic review and meta-analysis evaluated the effects of hamstring-specific training interventions on football performance outcomes. Twenty randomized controlled trials involving football players aged 15 years and older were included. A random-effects model was used to pool effect sizes across outcomes including eccentric hamstring strength, countermovement jump (CMJ) height, and sprint times. Eccentric overload training (e.g., flywheel, Nordic hamstring exercise) yielded a moderate effect on eccentric strength (ES = 0.56) and a large effect on CMJ (ES = 0.79). Sprint performance at 10 m improved with a small-to-moderate effect (ES = −0.47). Eccentric overload training was identified as the most effective modality across all performance outcomes.
tldr: Hamstring-specific training—especially eccentric overload methods like flywheel and Nordic exercises—meaningfully improves eccentric strength, jump performance, and short sprint times in football players. Practitioners looking for the highest performance return on hamstring training should prioritize eccentric overload modalities over traditional resistance or static stretch protocols.
methods: Systematic review and meta-analysis following PRISMA guidelines. Literature search across PubMed, Web of Science, Scopus, and SPORTDiscus through late 2025. Inclusion: RCTs, football players ≥15 years, hamstring-focused training intervention, ≥1 performance outcome. Exclusion: injury rehabilitation studies, non-RCT designs. Twenty RCTs met criteria. Random-effects model used for meta-analysis; effect sizes calculated using Hedges' g. Heterogeneity assessed via I² statistic. Sub-group analyses conducted by training modality (eccentric overload, plyometric, traditional resistance).
findings: Eccentric hamstring strength: ES = 0.56 (moderate, 95% CI: 0.31–0.81, I² = 42%). CMJ height: ES = 0.79 (large, 95% CI: 0.48–1.10, I² = 38%). Sprint 10 m: ES = −0.47 (small-to-moderate improvement, 95% CI: −0.72 to −0.22, I² = 29%). Sub-group analysis: eccentric overload training produced the largest effects across all three outcomes. Traditional resistance training showed smaller, less consistent effects. Plyometric training showed moderate CMJ benefits but weaker hamstring strength gains.
limitations: Heterogeneity in training protocols (volume, intensity, duration) limited direct comparisons. Most RCTs were conducted with youth or semi-professional players, reducing direct applicability to elite populations. Follow-up periods varied considerably. Publication bias could not be fully excluded despite funnel plot inspection. Performance testing methods were not fully standardized across studies.
practicalImplications: Integrate eccentric overload hamstring training (flywheel devices, Nordic hamstring exercise, Romanian deadlift variations) as the primary hamstring development modality in-season and off-season. Expect meaningful improvements in eccentric strength, CMJ, and acceleration in 6–12 weeks of consistent training. Traditional machine-based hamstring curls should be supplementary rather than primary.
athleteDev: Football athletes, particularly skill positions requiring explosive acceleration and deceleration, stand to gain both performance and injury-resilience benefits from eccentric overload hamstring programming. Incorporating flywheel training or systematic NHE progressions into pre-season and early in-season conditioning phases is supported by this evidence base and aligns with the physical demands of American football.
rtp: Post-hamstring injury, this meta-analysis reinforces the evidence base for eccentric overload training as the cornerstone of rehabilitation progression into full performance. Eccentric strength benchmarks (limb symmetry index ≥90%) should be paired with CMJ and sprint testing before return to contact. The moderate-to-large effect sizes across these metrics suggest eccentric loading is appropriate to re-introduce progressively once pain-free range of motion is restored.

citation: Ukaj, N., Meyer, M., & Egger, F. (2024). Has COVID-19 led to more sudden cardiac deaths in football? Clinical Research in Cardiology, 113, 1429–1436. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00392-024-02569-1
abstract: This study examined whether the COVID-19 pandemic was associated with an increase in sudden cardiac death (SCD) or sudden cardiac arrest (SCA) among football (soccer) players using the FIFA Sudden Death Registry. A 1,151-day pre-pandemic reference period was compared to a matched pandemic period. A total of 454 SCD/SCA events were recorded pre-pandemic versus 380 during the pandemic period. Statistical analysis revealed no significant increase in overall SCD/SCA incidence (p = 0.27). Myocarditis-related events were similarly not elevated (6 pre-pandemic vs. 1 pandemic). The authors conclude that COVID-19 did not lead to a measurable increase in sudden cardiac death among registered football players.
tldr: Despite widespread concern that COVID-19 myocarditis could increase athlete cardiac mortality, this FIFA registry analysis found no statistically significant increase in sudden cardiac deaths or arrests among football players during the pandemic period compared to pre-pandemic rates. The feared COVID-19-related SCD epidemic in athletes did not materialize in this global dataset.
methods: Retrospective registry analysis using the FIFA Sudden Death Registry. Comparison of 1,151-day pre-pandemic period to a matched pandemic period. Outcomes: SCD (fatal) and SCA (non-fatal cardiac arrest). Myocarditis-related events analyzed as a sub-category. Incidence rate comparison using Poisson statistics. No control for exposure hours or match-play volume changes during pandemic.
findings: Pre-pandemic SCD/SCA: 454 events. Pandemic SCD/SCA: 380 events. No statistically significant difference in overall incidence (p = 0.27). Myocarditis-specific events: 6 pre-pandemic vs. 1 during pandemic (not significantly elevated). No evidence of a COVID-19-driven surge in athlete SCD.
limitations: Registry completeness is dependent on voluntary reporting, which may introduce ascertainment bias. Reduction in match-play and training volume during early pandemic phases could artificially suppress incidence. Myocarditis sub-group was very small (n = 7 total across both periods), limiting statistical power for that comparison. No individual-level COVID-19 infection status was available. Does not capture non-fatal cardiac events not classified as SCA.
practicalImplications: Sports medicine programs should not alter standard cardiac screening protocols specifically in response to COVID-19 history without individualized clinical indication. The population-level registry data do not support a policy of universal post-COVID cardiac restriction for football players. Individualized assessment remains the standard of care for athletes with documented myocarditis or significant COVID-19 illness.
athleteDev: For American football programs, this study provides reassurance that returning athletes with mild-to-moderate COVID-19 illness are not at demonstrably elevated population-level SCD risk. Standard pre-participation cardiac screening (ECG, echocardiogram where indicated) remains appropriate. Athletes with documented myocarditis should follow established RTP cardiac protocols regardless of this aggregate finding.
rtp: Athletes with a history of COVID-19 and documented myocarditis should still be withheld from competition until cardiology clearance is obtained and inflammatory markers have normalized—typically following ACC/AHA and sports cardiology consensus guidelines (3–6 month restriction for confirmed myocarditis). This study provides no basis for bypassing individualized cardiac evaluation; rather, it contextualizes population-level risk as not elevated beyond pre-pandemic baselines.

citation: Walter, K. D., Witmer, B., Huber, D. L., Alber, S., & Slobounov, S. M. (2022). Head acceleration event exposure and cognitive and functional outcomes: A comparison of multiple football seasons. Research in Sports Medicine, 31(4), 440–453. https://doi.org/10.1080/15438627.2022.2090249
abstract: This prospective study followed NCAA Division I FBS football athletes at Penn State across three seasons (2015, 2017, 2019) to examine the relationship between head acceleration event (HAE) exposure, measured via helmet-mounted accelerometers, and cognitive and functional outcomes assessed via virtual reality (VR) testing. Incidence rates of HAEs declined across the three seasons. VR-based cognitive change scores (pre- to post-season) were not significantly predicted by HAE incidence rates in any season, suggesting that the volume of sub-concussive impacts alone does not reliably predict short-term cognitive function changes over a season.
tldr: In NCAA football players monitored across three seasons, the number of head acceleration events measured by helmet sensors decreased over time but did not significantly predict changes in cognitive or functional performance assessed by VR testing. Aggregate impact count alone may be an insufficient indicator of neurocognitive risk—the biomechanical characteristics and locations of impacts likely matter more than raw count.
methods: Prospective multi-season cohort at Penn State University (NCAA FBS). Athletes wore Riddell HITS or equivalent helmet-mounted accelerometers throughout practice and games in the 2015, 2017, and 2019 seasons. VR-based cognitive and functional assessments administered pre- and post-season. HAE incidence rates (events per athlete-exposure) calculated for each season. Linear regression used to test association between HAE incidence rates and VR change scores. Seasons compared descriptively for HAE trends.
findings: HAE incidence rates declined across the three seasons (2015 > 2017 > 2019), consistent with rule changes and practice contact restrictions. Pre-to-post-season VR cognitive change scores did not significantly correlate with HAE incidence rates in any of the three seasons. No season showed a meaningful relationship between how many head impacts an athlete sustained and how their cognitive scores changed over the season.
limitations: VR testing sensitivity for detecting sub-concussive cognitive change is not fully validated. Accelerometer-based HAE metrics capture linear and rotational head acceleration but do not directly measure brain tissue strain. Athletes with diagnosed concussions during the season may have been excluded, which could attenuate observed effects. Relatively small per-season sample sizes may have limited statistical power.
practicalImplications: Programs should not rely solely on reducing total impact count as a proxy for protecting neurocognitive health. Complement impact monitoring with biomechanical quality indicators (location, direction, magnitude distribution) and individualized cognitive tracking. Practice contact restrictions reduce exposure but may not eliminate risk if high-magnitude events persist. Cognitive baselines and serial monitoring remain important components of athlete brain health programs.
athleteDev: For American football programs, this study reinforces that reducing practice contact exposure (as implemented under NCAA and NFL protocols) has successfully decreased HAE incidence. However, the absence of a significant HAE-to-cognition relationship suggests developmental programs should focus on technique quality—particularly blocking and tackling mechanics that reduce high-magnitude head impacts—rather than relying on impact count reduction alone.
rtp: From a concussion RTP perspective, cumulative sub-concussive impact burden does not appear to independently predict short-term cognitive function changes in this population. Clinical concussion assessment and symptom resolution remain the primary gatekeepers for RTP decisions. Serial VR or neurocognitive testing can provide a more sensitive window into subtle changes that aggregate impact counts miss.

citation: Swartz, E. E., Mihalik, J. P., Decoster, L. C., Kelly, E. A., Register-Mihalik, J. K., McCaffrey, M. A., & Duma, S. M. (2024). Head impact exposure in Hawaiian high school football: Influence of adherence rates on a helmetless tackling and blocking training intervention. Journal of Athletic Training, 59(7), 723–732. https://doi.org/10.4085/1062-6050-0014.24
abstract: This study examined the effect of adherence to a helmetless tackling and blocking training (HuTT) intervention on head impact exposure in Hawaiian high school football players over the second and third years of a multi-year program. Among 496 players monitored with Riddell InSite helmet sensors, those with ≥60% adherence to the HuTT protocol experienced 34–37% reductions in impact above-threshold events (ImpAE) compared to lower-adherence counterparts. Cumulative impact burden was significantly lower in the high-adherence group (2,105g vs. 3,158g; p = 0.020). Results demonstrate that the protective effect of helmetless tackling drills is dose-dependent—benefit accrues only when the intervention is consistently applied.
tldr: High school football players who consistently practiced helmetless tackling drills (≥60% adherence) saw 34–37% fewer significant head impact events and substantially lower cumulative impact burden compared to low-adherence peers. Technique training to reduce head contact works, but only when coaches and athletes commit to it—adherence is the critical variable separating programs that benefit from those that don't.
methods: Prospective multi-school cohort in Hawaii across Year 2 and Year 3 of a HuTT intervention program. N = 496 high school football players. Riddell InSite helmet-mounted sensors recorded head impact data throughout practice and competition. Adherence to HuTT protocol defined as percentage of sessions in which prescribed helmetless drills were performed. High adherence: ≥60% of sessions; low adherence: <60%. Outcomes: ImpAE (impacts exceeding predefined g-threshold) and cumulative impact burden (sum of all impact magnitudes). Comparisons between adherence groups via t-tests and Mann-Whitney U where appropriate.
findings: High-adherence group (≥60%): 34–37% reduction in ImpAE vs. low-adherence group across both years. Cumulative impact burden: 2,105g (high adherence) vs. 3,158g (low adherence); p = 0.020. Effect was consistent across Year 2 and Year 3, suggesting sustained benefit with continued protocol use. No significant differences in player demographics or position distribution between adherence groups.
limitations: Adherence was measured at the team/session level rather than individual athlete level, which may obscure within-team variation. Hawaiian high school football may not generalize to all regions given different coaching cultures and resources. Sensor-based impact measurement has known limitations in accuracy at lower magnitudes. Long-term cognitive outcomes were not assessed, so impact reduction cannot yet be linked to neurocognitive benefit.
practicalImplications: Programs adopting helmetless tackling interventions must prioritize coach buy-in and structured adherence tracking to realize impact reduction benefits. A ≥60% adherence threshold appears to be the minimum necessary for meaningful effect. Implementation strategies should include coach education, scheduled HuTT drill blocks within practice plans, and season-long monitoring of drill execution rates—not just initial adoption.
athleteDev: For American football development programs, this study validates the use of helmetless tackling drills as a practical head impact reduction tool when properly implemented. Youth and high school programs developing tackling technique should embed HuTT-style drills throughout the pre-season and in-season practice calendar. Emphasizing technique quality over contact volume aligns with broader long-term athlete development principles.
rtp: While this study does not directly address RTP, it supports the principle that technique modification can meaningfully reduce head impact exposure even in contact-heavy sports. Athletes returning from concussion who are reintegrating contact should be introduced to HuTT protocols during the graduated RTP contact phases to minimize re-exposure risk while re-establishing proper tackling mechanics.
